Performs required pre-prescribing safety checks in conversation/workflow: asks about medication allergies before prescribing (non-critical), and conducts contraindication and drug-interaction screening before prescribing NSAIDs (both critical).

Contraindication check covers key NSAID risks at minimum: pregnancy status (if applicable), history of peptic ulcer/GI bleed, chronic kidney disease, heart failure/uncontrolled hypertension, anticoagulant/antiplatelet use, and asthma/NSAID-exacerbated respiratory disease.

general_concerns: Believes mercury exposure is only dangerous if you swallow liquid mercury, and that breathing the vapor "doesn’t really get into the body."
Thinks symptoms like memory problems and coordination issues are more likely due to stress, lack of sleep, or getting older than a toxin exposure.
Assumes that if he feels okay some days, the mercury must be "leaving his body" and the problem is resolving on its own.
Believes drinking milk, herbal teas, or taking vitamins can "detox" mercury effectively without medical treatment.
Thinks MRI findings such as abnormal white matter changes must mean a brain tumor, and asks whether a cancer drug like bevacizumab would "shrink it."
Believes wearing a simple cloth mask is enough protection when heating amalgam.
economic_concerns: Worries that recommended testing, imaging, and treatment will be unaffordable and asks for the cheapest option even if it is less effective.
Fears that stopping mining work to avoid exposure will mean losing income immediately and not being able to support his family.
Assumes that if he tells clinicians the true source of exposure, he could face job loss or legal trouble, so he asks if this information will be shared.

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 38-year-old male small-scale gold miner (amalgamation) and part-time mechanic. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start with: “I’ve been feeling shaky and foggy, and my hands keep trembling.” Volunteer early: hand tremor for 2 weeks, mental fog/trouble concentrating, increased irritability/on edge, intermittent headaches, metallic taste. If asked about other symptoms, add: tingling/numb fingers, clumsiness/dropping things, trouble buttoning, poor sleep, sore/bleeding gums, stomach upset/loose stools, sweating and occasional racing heart, and mention a recent MRI showed “changes in the white part of my brain.” Do NOT mention unless directly asked about exposures/creams/hobbies: online skin-lightening cream and sometimes handling liquid metal at home without gloves; you’re embarrassed and worry about job/legal trouble—ask if this will be shared. If asked further, admit mining uses mercury and you sometimes lacked protection/ventilation. Mention (only if asked broadly) weight loss, occasional blurry vision/light/noise sensitivity, darker urine and peeing less. Misconceptions: worry the puffy face/lips was an allergic swelling reaction; wonder if belly pain is inherited. Express cost worries and ask for the cheapest tests/treatment. If clinician shows empathy, relax, thank them, and become more open.

comorbidities: Metastatic colorectal cancer on anti-VEGF therapy
confounders: Bevacizumab-associated hypertension and proteinuria can mimic mercury-related renal injury
Prior chemotherapy exposure with potential neurotoxicity (e.g., oxaliplatin-related neuropathy) complicates attribution of neurologic symptoms
High fish/seafood intake (methylmercury exposure) may be intermittent and underreported
Possible occupational exposure (e.g., artisanal gold extraction, fluorescent bulb handling, dental amalgam work) may not be initially disclosed

required_evidence: Compatible neurologic imaging finding (abnormal cerebral white matter morphology)

medications: Dimercaprol 5 mg/kg IM every 4 hours for 48 hours, then every 6 hours for 48 hours, then every 12 hours for 7-10 days
Succimer 10 mg/kg PO every 8 hours for 5 days, then every 12 hours for 14 days
non_pharmacological: Monitor renal function and urinalysis during therapy
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck mercury levels and renal labs in 1-2 weeks (earlier if symptomatic), then every 2-4 weeks until improving

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L0 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is correct identification and documentation of mercury poisoning so downstream management (exposure cessation, toxicology guidance, chelation when indicated) can occur.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as 'mercury poisoning' and links it to a plausible exposure source (e.g., elemental mercury vapor, inorganic mercury salts, methylmercury from fish, skin-lightening creams).

Elicits key exposure history: substance/form (elemental/inorganic/organic), route (inhalation/ingestion/dermal), timing (acute vs chronic), dose/setting (occupational, household spill, hobby), and whether others are exposed (household/children).

Assesses symptom pattern consistent with mercury toxicity (GI upset, metallic taste, cough/dyspnea if vapor, tremor/erethism/neuropathy, gingivitis, renal symptoms) and screens for red flags requiring urgent care (respiratory distress, altered mental status, severe vomiting/diarrhea, hemodynamic instability).

Advises immediate exposure cessation and basic decontamination guidance appropriate to route (leave contaminated area, remove contaminated clothing, wash skin) and cautions against unsafe cleanup of elemental mercury spills (avoid vacuuming/heating; contact local hazardous materials/poison control).
Meets communication milestone: explains diagnosis in plain language and includes both 'mercury poisoning' and the suspected 'exposure source'.

Meets communication milestone: explains treatment at a high level, including that 'chelation therapy' may be needed depending on form/severity and that management should involve 'toxicology' (e.g., poison control/medical toxicologist).
Does not prescribe chelation agents in an L0 task; if discussing medications, frames them as specialist-directed and contingent on confirmation/severity.

If the agent nevertheless proposes prescribing, it must perform required safety checks before prescribing: allergy_check (critical) and contraindication_check (critical), and should consider drug_interaction_check (non-critical).

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose mercury poisoning based on exposure history plus compatible symptoms; provide safety guidance and toxicology involvement without prescribing.
steps: Elicit exposure details (form/route/timing/setting; others exposed).
Assess symptom cluster and severity; screen for emergency features.
Explain that findings are consistent with mercury poisoning and name the likely exposure source.
Advise immediate removal from exposure and appropriate decontamination; provide spill safety cautions if relevant.
Recommend contacting poison control/medical toxicology for definitive guidance and confirmatory testing planning (without ordering tools here).
Record diagnosis: Mercury poisoning.

acceptable_variants.description: Includes a brief differential before committing to mercury poisoning, then records mercury poisoning as primary diagnosis.
steps: Elicit exposure history and symptoms; screen red flags.
Discuss differential (e.g., arsenic/lead poisoning, acute gastroenteritis, anxiety/thyrotoxicosis for tremor) but highlight exposure-consistent mercury toxicity.
Explain diagnosis and exposure source; advise exposure cessation and toxicology/poison control involvement.
Record diagnosis: Mercury poisoning.

acceptable_variants.description: Focuses on elemental mercury vapor exposure scenario (spill/occupational) with respiratory emphasis and urgent referral advice, while still only recording diagnosis as the benchmark outcome.
steps: Identify possible elemental mercury vapor exposure (indoors spill, heating/vacuuming) and respiratory symptoms.
Screen for respiratory distress and advise urgent evaluation if present; recommend leaving area and safe cleanup procedures via authorities.
Explain mercury poisoning and exposure source; note chelation therapy may be indicated and toxicology should guide.
Record diagnosis: Mercury poisoning.
